sources.name: The Catholic Encyclopedia

sources.title: The Catholic Encyclopedia

sources.name: New American Bible

sources.title: New American Bible

sources.name: Liturgical Arts Journal
sources.description: A journal dedicated to the study of liturgical art and architecture, often featuring articles on the symbolism and history of liturgical objects like the thurible.
sources.title: Liturgical Arts Journal

sources.name: Joseph Ratzinger, The Spirit of the Liturgy
sources.description: A theological exploration of the meaning and significance of Christian liturgy by Pope Benedict XVI, discussing the role of symbols and rituals.
sources.title: Joseph Ratzinger, The Spirit of the Liturgy

sources.name: Early Church Fathers Writings
sources.description: Collection of writings from early Christian leaders, providing insights into the development of Christian practices and beliefs regarding incense and worship.
sources.title: Early Church Fathers Writings

longDescription: The thurible, also called a censer, is a ritual vessel designed to hold burning incense during religious ceremonies. The Christian thurible typically consists of a metal bowl suspended from chains, with a perforated cover that allows aromatic smoke to escape while containing the burning materials. This design enables the thurifer (person swinging the censer) to direct smoke throughout a sacred space through rhythmic swinging motions.
name: Thurible

keyMyths.title: The Use of Incense in the Temple
keyMyths.description: In the Old Testament, incense was a key part of Temple worship, symbolizing prayer rising to God. The burning of incense in the thurible connects Christian ritual to this ancient tradition, signifying a continuous link to the divine.

keyMyths.title: The Incense of the Apocalypse
keyMyths.description: The Book of Revelation describes angels offering incense with the prayers of the saints upon the golden altar before the throne of God. This imagery reinforces the thurible's role in elevating human prayers to the heavenly realm.

keyMyths.title: Incense as Purification
keyMyths.description: Throughout the Bible, smoke is associated with purification and cleansing. The use of incense in the thurible symbolizes the purification of the space and the worshippers present, creating a sacred atmosphere.

keyMyths.title: The Offering of Incense by the Magi
keyMyths.description: The Magi's gift of frankincense to the infant Jesus is interpreted as a recognition of his divinity and priestly role. This event highlights the sacred and valuable nature of incense and its association with Christ.

keyMyths.title: The Cleansing Power of Incense During Plague
keyMyths.description: Historically, incense was used during outbreaks of plague and disease, believed to purify the air and ward off illness. This perceived power contributed to its symbolic association with protection and divine intervention.
keyMyths.source: Various historical accounts of plague outbreaks and liturgical responses
